caveats: a: No public occupation distribution or time-on-task study was found for this six-digit code or narrowed lens.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis; high commodity pass-through can suppress the apparent labor intensity.
rho: The evidence is occupational and cotton-specific rather than a measured adoption rate across the narrowed multi-commodity lens.
rho: Cybersecurity, data entitlements, and integration with warehouse and trade-finance systems can delay deployment.
e: Public data do not reveal the six-digit code's firm or receipt mix by commodity.
e: The frozen 381-firm LMM estimate is margin-bridged using a broad distributor margin; commodity turnover and inventory gains can make sales a poor EBITDA proxy.
s5: BizBuySell observations skew toward smaller publicly marketed businesses and are far broader than this lens.
s5: Workforce age is not owner age, sale intent, or evidence that customer and supplier relationships will transfer.
q: No source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through in these commodities.
q: Commodity price and inventory gains can overwhelm small operating-cost changes and obscure true retention.
d5: Commodity weights inside NAICS 424590 are unknown, making any blend highly uncertain.
d5: USDA long-term projections were completed in October 2024, while the hemp and trade snapshots are newer but short-horizon and volatile.
d5: Underlying commodity volume can grow even if producers and processors bypass merchants.
o: Operator need varies materially by commodity and by domestic versus export transaction.
o: Software may reduce local headcount while preserving only a thin accountable merchant layer; this primitive measures operator presence, not labor intensity.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Merchants generally buy raw materials on their own account from farms or assemblers and resell to processors, manufacturers, exporters, or other wholesalers. The recurring service embedded in the spread is aggregation, quality matching, inventory and price-risk management, documentation, and delivery coordination rather than a separately billed service fee.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 424590 combines living-animal, highly perishable, regulated cannabis, and storable industrial or food raw-material models. The lens keeps storable nonliving raw-material merchants because their workflows, risks, and revenue model can be assessed coherently; the narrowing is based on business-model incompatibility rather than attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Live chicks, bees, horses, and mules; bovine semen; sod; cannabis businesses whose legality or product model prevents a comparable national screen; captive procurement units; pure speculative positions without repeat external customers; owner-only books that cannot transfer; shells; and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market merchants of storable, nonliving farm raw materials such as raw cotton, hemp and other fibers, hides and pelts, leaf tobacco, raw nuts, oil kernels, raw spices, and raw sugar, where recurring sourcing, aggregation, grading, market access, documentation, and logistics are provided to external counterparties by a transferable team.

a.bridge: BLS describes national wholesale-sales and purchasing occupations, not the wage-weighted occupation mix or task shares of NAICS 424590. I mapped prospecting, quote preparation, customer and supplier communications, market and inventory analysis, contract and order administration, logistics coordination, and document extraction to AI-exposed work, while leaving physical sampling, grading oversight, warehousing, relationship negotiation, and accountable trading largely unexposed. The mapping may overstate exposure in small field-based assemblers and understate it in export-oriented merchants with document-heavy workflows.
caveats: No public occupation distribution or time-on-task study was found for this six-digit code or narrowed lens.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis; high commodity pass-through can suppress the apparent labor intensity.
a.change_evidence: Obtain a wage-weighted role roster and two-week task logs from at least fifteen cotton, hemp, hide, nut, and tobacco merchants, split among sales, buyers, quality, traffic, warehouse, trade finance, compliance, and management; code current automation before estimating residual AI exposure.
a.rationale: The included merchants coordinate large volumes of market data, specifications, contracts, shipments, and counterparties, creating substantial information work. Physical quality, commodity risk, supplier relationships, and exception resolution remain labor-intensive, preventing a majority-task estimate.

d5.bridge: USDA measures or projects commodity production and trade, not constant-quality service demand for the narrowed merchant population. Upland-cotton production rises from 15.4 million bales in 2026/27 to 15.8 million in 2031/32, with exports rising from 13.4 million to 14.1 million; 2025 hemp production and value also grew sharply, while current hide exports weaken and tobacco is mature. I use modest aggregate growth as the base, with a wide range because the unknown commodity weights could bias the result in either direction and channel bypass may separate merchant demand from underlying volume.
caveats: Commodity weights inside NAICS 424590 are unknown, making any blend highly uncertain.
USDA long-term projections were completed in October 2024, while the hemp and trade snapshots are newer but short-horizon and volatile.
Underlying commodity volume can grow even if producers and processors bypass merchants.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain 424590 receipts and gross-margin shares by product from Economic Census microdata or a custom Census tabulation, then combine commodity-specific USDA production, import, export, and merchant-channel forecasts through 2031 with constant-quality service units.
d5.rationale: Cotton, the clearest large storable segment, has a modest positive volume path, and newer hemp categories are expanding from a volatile small base. Hides, tobacco, and some raw agricultural products face weak or uneven demand, so the blended service basket is closer to stable than high growth.

caveats: Public data do not reveal the six-digit code's firm or receipt mix by commodity.
The frozen 381-firm LMM estimate is margin-bridged using a broad distributor margin; commodity turnover and inventory gains can make sales a poor EBITDA proxy.
e.change_evidence: Construct a firm-level census from the 2022 Economic Census type-of-operation data, commercial business directories, USDA program lists, state licenses, and company websites; classify product, recurring-customer behavior, service content, management depth, and normalized EBITDA for each candidate.
e.rationale: The code itself is a residual basket, and Census defines merchant wholesalers as buying on their own account. Only storable nonliving segments with recurring counterparties and real service functions fit the coherent lens; live-animal, sod, legally fragmented cannabis, pure principal speculation, and owner-only relationship books do not. The base assumes fewer than one-third of frozen LMM-band firms are both in-scope and transferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 424590 ($1,068,761,277) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 424590 ($23,930,500,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,788,795 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 424590); class <5: 590 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,556,039 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $290,622 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 150 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,231,873 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,049,664 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,376,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,520,936 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 107 firms, avg revenue/firm $55,368,935 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,295,448 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $185,037,087 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $21,038,717 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $511,033,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,104,553 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 381

caveats: Operator need varies materially by commodity and by domestic versus export transaction.
Software may reduce local headcount while preserving only a thin accountable merchant layer; this primitive measures operator presence, not labor intensity.
o.change_evidence: Measure direct producer-to-processor sales and platform-only transactions by commodity using USDA Market News, cotton electronic-record access, trade-platform data, and interviews with growers, processors, warehouses, lenders, insurers, and merchants; determine who retains title, credit, and quality-claim liability.
o.rationale: Digital platforms and standardized quality data can support direct matching, but most remaining volume still requires title-taking or an accountable coordinator for supplier qualification, grade and specification matching, inventory, trade credit, claims, and physical delivery. USDA cotton data make remote trading easier but also show that trusted classification and authorized access remain integral to marketing.

caveats: No source directly measures AI-benefit pass-through in these commodities.
Commodity price and inventory gains can overwhelm small operating-cost changes and obscure true retention.
q.change_evidence: Analyze five years of lot- and customer-level gross margin, bid history, benchmark basis, rebates, freight, quality claims, churn, and contract renewals at merchants before and after specific workflow automation; compare against non-adopting regional peers.
q.rationale: Benefits are not contractually passed through because merchants earn a commodity spread, yet customers can observe benchmark prices and switch sources. Competitive bids, electronic markets, renewal repricing, and rival adoption should return a substantial portion of savings to counterparties. Differentiated quality assurance, inventory availability, trade credit, and reliable logistics support higher retention.

rho.bridge: BLS expects AI and automation to affect broad wholesale sales and procurement work but does not measure five-year implementation in farm raw-material merchants. I translate that direction into adoption after discounting for fragmented legacy systems, multilingual trade documents, model errors in specifications and prices, and integration with inventory and logistics. This may be conservative for merchants already using standardized digital cotton data and optimistic for small analog assemblers.
caveats: The evidence is occupational and cotton-specific rather than a measured adoption rate across the narrowed multi-commodity lens.
Cybersecurity, data entitlements, and integration with warehouse and trade-finance systems can delay deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Collect implementation case studies and pre/post labor-hour data from commodity-trading and wholesale ERP vendors, electronic cotton-data users, trade-document platforms, and merchants; audit error rates and human-review requirements by workflow.
rho.rationale: Document extraction, market summarization, quote and contract drafting, customer-service assistance, inventory exception flags, and shipment-status workflows can be implemented with human approval. Quality and title errors can be expensive, while volatile commodity positions require accountable controls, so exposed work will not be fully automated.

caveats: BizBuySell observations skew toward smaller publicly marketed businesses and are far broader than this lens.
Workforce age is not owner age, sale intent, or evidence that customer and supplier relationships will transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Create a 2021-2026 event history from PitchBook, Capital IQ, BizBuySell, trade publications, UCC and state corporate filings, and USDA or commodity-association membership changes; have sector brokers classify external control transfers versus internal reorganizations, family succession, and asset-only closures.
s5.rationale: Wholesale/distribution businesses demonstrably sell, and Census shows an aging wholesale workforce, but neither source provides a denominator-based control-transfer rate for eligible farm raw-material merchants. The estimate allows for retirement and ordinary sale activity while discounting internal succession, trade-house ownership, failed processes, working-capital intensity, and key-person customer or supplier books.

principal_driver: Document-heavy, specification-sensitive recurring trades offer practical AI assistance around physical commodities.
principal_weakness: The code's extreme product heterogeneity and own-account merchant economics make eligibility, demand, and normalized EBITDA unusually uncertain.

run_meta.title: Other Farm Product Raw Material Merchant Wholesalers

sources.supports: Defines the residual farm-raw-material merchant category and lists examples including live chicks, hemp, horses and mules, hides, raw cotton, nuts, pelts, leaf tobacco, sod, wool, cocoa beans, sugar, spices, and cannabis, establishing product heterogeneity.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 424590 Other Farm Product Raw Material Merchant Wholesalers

sources.supports: Lists prospecting, customer communication, needs analysis, price and availability responses, negotiation, contracts, orders, and follow-up; says online wholesale mostly complements face-to-face selling and AI/chatbots may limit employment growth.
sources.title: Wholesale and Manufacturing Sales Representatives

sources.supports: Describes sales-record and inventory analysis, supplier identification, demand monitoring, contracts, and ordering; says procurement tasks will continue to automate and may use AI while humans remain needed for supply-chain and negotiation work.
sources.title: Purchasing Managers, Buyers, and Purchasing Agents

sources.supports: Defines a merchant wholesaler as buying merchandise on its own account and reselling it, and a farm-products assembler as purchasing directly from farmers; clarifies the spread-based operating model.
sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Frequently Asked Questions - Wholesale

sources.supports: Projects upland-cotton production from 15.4 million bales in 2026/27 to 15.8 million in 2031/32 and exports from 13.4 million to 14.1 million, while domestic mill use remains flat at 1.79 million bales.
sources.title: USDA Agricultural Projections to 2034

sources.supports: Reports 2025 U.S. hemp value of $739 million, up 64 percent; open-field planted acreage up 9 percent, harvested acreage up 34 percent, fiber production up 11 percent, and grain production up 112 percent from 2024.
sources.title: National Hemp Report, April 16, 2026

sources.supports: States that USDA grades every U.S. cotton bale, issues bale-level electronic data used in marketing and sourcing, and measures color, trash, micronaire, length, uniformity, and strength with instrument and visual controls.
sources.title: Cotton Classing Services

sources.supports: Reports observed sales of wholesale and distribution businesses during 2021-2025, including annual median prices and a five-year median revenue of $1.379 million and owner earnings of $236,484; the population is broader and smaller than this lens.
sources.title: Wholesale/Distribution Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks

sources.supports: Reports that over 40 percent of wholesale-trade employment in 2022 was at firms where at least one-quarter of workers were over 55, up from 14 percent in 2000; this concerns workforces, not owners.
sources.title: Firms in Production Sectors and Northern States Have Some of the Highest Shares of Older Workers

sources.supports: Forecasts fiscal-2026 export values of $0.6 billion for hides, skins, and furs versus $0.727 billion in 2025; $4.8 billion for cotton versus $4.910 billion; $11.0 billion for tree nuts versus $10.535 billion; and $1.6 billion for tobacco and products versus $1.5 billion.
sources.title: Outlook for U.S. Agricultural Trade: May 2026

title: Other Farm Product Raw Material Merchant Wholesalers
